title: dependency_poisoning
description: A threat actor publishes a typosquatted npm package mimicking a popular internal library; developers inadvertently install it, introducing a credential-stealing payload.

timeline.title: Malicious Package Installed
timeline.description: A developer installs 'internall-utils' (typosquatting the internal 'internal-utils' package) from the public npm registry. SIEM detects unexpected outbound connections from a developer workstation.

timeline.title: Credential Theft Payload Active
timeline.description: The malicious package's postinstall script reads environment variables and SSH keys, exfiltrating them to an external server.

timeline.title: Scope Identified
timeline.description: Four developer workstations installed the malicious package over the past week. CI/CD pipelines may also have executed the payload.

timeline.title: Package Removed, Credentials Rotated
timeline.description: Malicious package removed from all systems. All potentially exposed credentials, SSH keys, and API tokens rotated.

timeline.title: npm Scope Configured
timeline.description: Internal packages migrated to a scoped namespace (@company/internal-utils). Private npm registry configured to shadow public registry for internal scopes.

timeline.title: Supply Chain Security Hardened
timeline.description: Package integrity verification enabled (npm audit signatures). Developer security training on dependency hygiene completed.

node-1.title: Typosquatted npm Package Installed
node-1.situation: SIEM alerts on unexpected outbound HTTPS connections from a developer workstation to 198.51.100.200 immediately after an npm install. Investigation shows the installed package 'internall-utils' is a typosquatted version of your internal 'internal-utils' library, containing a credential-stealing postinstall script.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Block the external IP at the firewall, remove the malicious package from all developer systems, and initiate a search for all systems that installed it.

options.label: B
options.text: Report the package to npm security and wait for it to be removed from the registry before taking internal action.

options.label: C
options.text: Only remove the package from the affected workstation and assume other developers would not have made the same typo.

node-2.title: Assess Credential Theft Scope
node-2.situation: Four developer workstations installed the malicious package. The postinstall script exfiltrated environment variables, SSH private keys, and npm auth tokens. CI/CD pipelines may have also executed the payload.
node-2.question: How do you scope and contain the credential theft?

options.label: A
options.text: Rotate all credentials, SSH keys, API tokens, and npm auth tokens from affected systems, revoke and regenerate CI/CD pipeline secrets, and audit pipeline logs for evidence of execution.

options.label: B
options.text: Only rotate the credentials from the initially identified workstation and ask developers to change their npm passwords.

node-3.title: Prevent Future Dependency Confusion
node-3.situation: The attack succeeded because your internal package name was not registered in the public npm registry, allowing a typosquatted version to be installed. You need to close this attack vector.
node-3.question: How do you prevent dependency confusion attacks?

options.label: A
options.text: Migrate all internal packages to a scoped npm namespace (e.g., @company/), register the scoped packages in a private registry configured to proxy public packages, and pin all dependency versions with integrity hashes.

options.label: B
options.text: Register the unscoped internal package names in the public npm registry as placeholders without implementing a private registry.

node-4.title: Implement Dependency Security Scanning
node-4.situation: Controls are in place to prevent confusion attacks. Now you need to detect malicious or vulnerable packages before they reach developer systems or production.
node-4.question: What automated security controls do you deploy?

options.label: A
options.text: Integrate npm audit and a software composition analysis (SCA) tool into CI/CD to block packages with known vulnerabilities or unexpected postinstall scripts, and enable npm package signing verification.

options.label: B
options.text: Rely on developers manually reviewing packages before installation — no automated scanning is required.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The dependency poisoning attack was contained. All compromised credentials were rotated, internal packages migrated to scoped namespaces, and a private registry with SCA scanning now prevents future typosquatting and dependency confusion attacks.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
